channel.title: Ahmed Elhossiny
channel.description: Postdoctoral Fellow in Computational Oncology at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center

item.title: The EM Algorithm, Part 3: The Rigorous Treatment
item.description:
Three-part series. 1. The Intuition — the idea, no heavy math. 2. Math with Intuition — every equation, explained in plain words. 3. The Rigorous Treatment (you are here) — proofs, calculus, and the formal guarantees.

This part proves what the earlier parts asserted: the ELBO/KL decomposition, the optimality of the E-step, the closed-form M-step, the monotonicity theorem, and convergence to a stationary point. The two-coin model is the running concrete instance.

1. The latent-variable model

Observed data , latent variables , parameters . The model specifies a joint , and the observed-data likelihood marginalizes the latents:

Coin instance. , , , so with , , .

The difficulty is structural: contains , a log-of-a-sum, which is not separable in and admits no closed-form maximizer.

2. Jensen’s inequality and a corollary

Theorem (Jensen). If is concave and a random variable with finite mean, then , with equality iff is almost surely constant or is affine on the support of . For a finite convex combination, with , .

Corollary (Gibbs’ inequality; non-negativity of KL). For distributions over the same finite set,

Proof. Apply Jensen to the concave :

Equality in Jensen requires constant on the support, i.e. .

Note that this is the same inequality that makes the ELBO a lower bound — seen once as and once as .

3. The ELBO and the exact decomposition

For any distribution with the same support as the posterior, define the evidence lower bound

Theorem (decomposition). For every and ,

Proof (one experiment; sum over at the end). Since and using ,

Insert inside the log and split the resulting product:

Because , we recover for all , with equality iff for all .

NoteAside: the two-term form of the ELBO and the notation “:=”.

Splitting and reading each sum as an expectation gives

The first term is the -dependent expected complete-data log-likelihood (Section 5); the second is the negative entropy , independent of . The symbol “” (as in ) means “the left side defines the new name on the colon side” — it is an equality by definition, not a derived identity. ( is the same with the new name on the left.)

4. The E-step: optimal q

Proposition. For fixed , , and at this optimum .

Proof. In the decomposition , the term does not depend on . Hence maximizing over is equivalent to minimizing the (non-negative) KL sum, which attains its floor of exactly at .

Coin instance (responsibilities). By Bayes’ rule, with the binomial coefficient cancelling,

5. The M-step: maximizing Q

With fixed (entropy term constant in ), maximizing over is maximizing

Coin instance. Using ,

Setting this to zero — and noting is a constant here (frozen by the E-step) — gives a linear equation with the closed-form root

The two updates are decoupled because ’s terms in carry coefficient and vanish under .

NoteLemma: the binomial derivative used above.

For ,

This is what makes both the above and the in Section 6 collapse to factors of .

6. Why no closed form exists for ℓ directly

It is instructive to differentiate the intractable and see precisely where it jams. With ,

The chain rule on the outer drops a whose denominator contains both and . Equivalently, the prefactor is itself a nonlinear function of . Setting therefore yields

an implicit, transcendental, coupled system: appears on the left and (through ) nonlinearly on the right, and the twin equation for mirrors it. No finite algebraic formula solves it.

EM’s resolution is exactly the comparison of Sections 5 and 6: freezing at converts the same stationarity equation into a linear one with a closed-form root. EM is thus a fixed-point iteration for the coupled score equations, solved by alternation; a fixed point satisfies the true (Section 8).

7. The monotonicity theorem

Theorem. Let (E-step) and where (M-step). Then

Proof. Write the decomposition with and abbreviate (the -free entropy), so that . Then

Since is non-decreasing and (here) bounded above, the sequence converges.

8. Convergence to a stationary point

Define the gap as a function of with fixed: . By construction , so is a global minimizer of , whence (assuming differentiability) . Differentiating the decomposition at gives the tangency identity

Consequently, if is a fixed point of EM, then maximizes , so , and by tangency : every EM fixed point is a stationary point of the marginal log-likelihood. (EM may converge to a local maximum or saddle, not necessarily the global maximum; initialization matters.)

The tangency also explains the rate: near each bound peaks almost directly above its contact point, so steps shrink and the tail is linear (slow).

The figure (a 1-D slice fixing ) shows each amber ELBO touching at a green point where equals the posterior (KL ), the M-step reaching its peak (orange diamond), and the reopened KL gap between each diamond and the next contact point above it.

NoteAside: EM as coordinate ascent on a single objective.

Both steps maximize the same function : the E-step ascends in (with fixed) to $q = $ posterior; the M-step ascends in (with fixed) to . EM is therefore block-coordinate ascent on , which converges to a stationary point of ; by the decomposition, stationary points of in project to stationary points of in .

NoteAside: generalization beyond two coins.

Nothing above is special to binomials. For any model with complete-data likelihood in an exponential family, the M-step replaces sufficient statistics by their posterior expectations under (expected sufficient statistics), and the E-step computes those expectations. Gaussian mixtures, HMMs (Baum–Welch), and factor analysers are all instances: same decomposition, same monotonicity proof, same tangency-based stationarity.

9. Numerical appendix

EM on , , , seed . Responsibilities are computed at ; the row shows the resulting and the new .

(per experiment)
0 0.7073 0.5765
1 0.7435 0.5691
2 0.7671 0.5500
3 — 0.7826 0.5350
4 — 0.7908 0.5265
5 — 0.7944 0.5225
converged

Worked first update (verification):

The log-likelihood column is monotone non-decreasing (Section 7); is non-monotone (it overshoots to then relaxes), consistent with the theorem guaranteeing ascent only in , not in individual coordinates.

← Part 2 — Math with Intuition | Back to Part 1 — The Intuition

item.title: The EM Algorithm, Part 2: Math with Intuition
item.description:
Three-part series. 1. The Intuition — the idea, no heavy math. 2. Math with Intuition (you are here) — every equation, explained in plain words. 3. The Rigorous Treatment — proofs, calculus, and the formal guarantees.

This part keeps the two-coin example from Part 1 and builds the full machinery: the marginal likelihood, the obstacle that makes it hard, Jensen’s inequality, the ELBO, the KL gap, and the geometry that guarantees EM works.

Notation

Per experiment : = heads observed (data), = which coin was used (latent). Parameters ; mixing priors ; flips. One coin gives a binomial:

For brevity write (and similarly ).

The thing we actually want to maximize

We only see the , so the quantity to maximize is the marginal log-likelihood, summing the latent coin out:

Reading it from the inside out:

  • The joint — “pick a coin, then flip it.” For the two coins: and .
  • The inner sum is marginalization: you can’t see the coin, so you add up over every value it could take. The result is the per-experiment marginal, a mixture of two binomials:

  • The puts it on log scale; the outer adds across the (independent) experiments.
NoteDo we get the marginal first then take the log, or take the log of the sum then add up?

Both — they’re the same number, via one log identity. There are two sums: an inner sum over the coin (builds each marginal) and an outer combination over experiments. Because experiments are independent, the whole-dataset likelihood is a product of the marginals:

Take one log of that product, and turns it into a sum of logs:

So “multiply the marginals, then log” and “log each marginal, then add” land on the identical . In practice we always use the sum-of-logs form (adding beats multiplying tiny probabilities that underflow).

The obstacle: log of a sum

Look at the shape of the right-hand side: a wrapped around a sum.

You can’t distribute it — — so the two unknowns , stay tangled inside that log, with no clean derivative and no closed-form maximizer. This single obstacle is the entire reason EM exists.

Contrast the easy world: if the coin labels were known, the log would sit on a single binomial, and — a tidy, separable expression you can maximize in closed form. The sum is what ruins this.

Slipping in a helper distribution q

Introduce a free distribution over the latent coin — just two numbers and summing to 1. “Free” means we choose it, and the algebra below holds for any choice.

Multiply and divide inside the sum by (which equals multiplying by 1):

That last equality is just the definition of expectation, . So the marginal is now “log of an expectation,” .

NoteWhy does dividing by q turn the sum into an expectation, and what does “free” mean?

An expectation is the pattern “sum over outcomes of (probability) × (value).” By writing we forced the marginal into exactly that shape, with as the weighting and as the function being averaged. The two copies of don’t cancel conceptually: the front one is a probability weight, the bottom one is part of the function — and that separation is what sets up the next step.

“Free” means is a variable we control, constrained only by being a distribution (, sums to 1, and nonzero wherever the joint is). The whole point of slipping it in is to reshape the stubborn into a that Jensen’s inequality can act on.

Jensen’s inequality

For a concave function (like ), the function of an average is at least the average of the function:

Geometrically: a concave curve bulges above its chords, so evaluating the curve at the average (a point on the curve) sits at or above averaging two curve-heights (a point on the chord).

The green dot is “function of the average” (on the curve); the orange dot is “average of the function” (on the chord); the vertical distance is the Jensen gap, which is zero only when the two inputs coincide.

Applying this to our — concave , so push it inside at the cost of an inequality:

That right-hand side is the ELBO, written . The now sits outside the sum, on single product terms — exactly the splittable, differentiable form we wanted.

NoteDoesn’t splitting that log violate log(a+b) ≠ log(a)+log(b)?

No — the thing inside this log is a quotient, not a sum. splits over and freely (); it’s only that’s forbidden. The sum over is now outside the log (it’s the expectation’s weighting), while inside each log there’s just one fraction . Jensen’s whole job was to relocate the sum from inside the log (forbidden) to outside it (fine) — which is precisely what makes this split legal.

The exact gap is a KL divergence

The inequality has an exact bookkeeping form. For any :

The gap between the true log-likelihood and the ELBO is exactly the KL divergence between your chosen and the posterior . The KL has two properties that drive everything:

  • always — so the ELBO is always a lower bound;
  • iff — so the bound is tight exactly when is the posterior.

On the coins, with and posterior , the KL is just

a concrete number measuring how far your working belief is from the row’s actual posterior — zero when they match.

The two steps, and the geometry

Rearranging the boxed identity, . Hold fixed: is a constant, so maximizing the ELBO over = minimizing the KL over — a seesaw. Both bottom out at $q = $ posterior.

E-step (touch). Set , the posterior at your current spot. This zeroes the KL at , so the ELBO rises up to touch the true curve right where you stand.

M-step (climb). Freeze that and maximize the ELBO over — . Since the bound is easy and decoupled, this is a closed-form update.

Why climbing the bound raises the truth — chain three facts:

End to end: . The true log-likelihood never decreases.

Because the dome reopens a gap once you step off , you rebuild it each round — a staircase of bounds walking up the true curve:

The steps shrink near the top: as you approach the optimum, each bound’s peak sits almost directly above its touch point (the bound is tangent to there), so there’s less left to climb — which is both why EM converges and why its tail is slow.

NoteWhy does the bound curve down instead of following the true curve?

Because the in the bound is frozen at its value during the climb. The gap is , which compares your frozen to the posterior at the current . At they match (KL = 0, touch). Step away and the posterior shifts while does not, so KL grows and the dome sags below the true curve by exactly that amount. If you let track the posterior at every , KL would be zero everywhere and the dome would be the true log-likelihood — with all its intractability back. The sag is the price of making the bound easy; re-touching each round is how EM keeps paying it.

NoteHow can q be the “true posterior” if I don’t know the right θ yet?

is the true posterior given your current , not given the correct . The posterior under the right parameters, , is indeed uncomputable — but EM never needs it. It only ever computes , which uses the concrete numbers you’re holding. Within one iteration EM treats as if it were correct: “suppose the biases are ; then the mathematically correct belief about the coins is exactly this posterior.” That conditional is exactly true regardless of whether is right. Feeding this self-consistent-but-slightly-wrong posterior into the M-step yields a less wrong ; at convergence, current has become and the two notions of “true posterior” finally coincide.

The E-step and M-step on the coins

E-step (responsibilities). Setting to the posterior gives, by Bayes’ rule,

M-step (weighted MLE). Maximizing the ELBO over reduces to maximizing the expected complete-data log-likelihood,

whose maximizers are soft heads-over-flips counts:

Each row contributes to each coin in proportion to its responsibility — the “soft counting” from Part 1, now derived.

NoteIs q(z=A) the same kind of thing as the prior π_A?

Same type (both probabilities about which coin), different role. is the prior — the chance coin A is picked before seeing the flips (fixed, same for every row, here ). is meant to be a posterior — the belief about this row’s coin after folding in its flips (per-row, carries the subscript ). They’re linked by Bayes’ rule, with sitting inside the formula for the best : start at the prior , update on 9 heads, land at . The data is exactly what separates the two.

Watching the bounds touch and climb, for real

Running EM on the five experiments from a seed of , produces this trajectory:

And here is the actual ELBO geometry (1-D slice fixing , varying ). The blue curve is the true log-likelihood; each amber dome is a real ELBO built at one iterate:

Read it left to right:

  • Each amber dome touches the blue curve at a green dot (where was set to the posterior, so KL = 0).
  • The M-step climbs the dome to its peak — the orange diamond.
  • Look directly above each diamond: the blue curve is higher. That vertical gap is the KL reopening, and it’s why the next green dot (the next E-step) sits up on blue, not down at the diamond. You gained more true likelihood than the bound alone promised.
  • The green dots march monotonically up blue toward .

The loop, compactly

Repeat until stops moving.

← Part 1 — The Intuition | Continue to Part 3 — The Rigorous Treatment →

item.title: The EM Algorithm, Part 1: The Intuition
item.description:
This is a three-part series. 1. The Intuition (you are here) — the idea, no heavy math. 2. Math with Intuition — every equation, explained in plain words. 3. The Rigorous Treatment — proofs, calculus, and the formal guarantees.

The problem: a chicken-and-egg puzzle

The Expectation–Maximization (EM) algorithm exists to solve one frustrating situation: you want to estimate some parameters, but to estimate them you’d need to know a hidden label, and to know the hidden label you’d need the parameters. Each one would unlock the other, and you have neither.

EM breaks the loop by guessing, then refining.

The running example: two coins

Imagine two biased coins, A and B, with unknown head-probabilities and . Someone runs five experiments. In each one they secretly grab one of the two coins, flip it 10 times, and write down only the number of heads — not which coin they used:

Experiment Heads (out of 10)
1 5
2 9
3 8
4 4
5 7

Your goal is to recover and . The annoying part is the missing label — which coin produced each row. That hidden label is the latent variable.

Why it’s circular

  • If a kind soul told you the coin for each row, this would be trivial: pool all the A-rows, count heads over flips, done.
  • Conversely, if someone handed you and , you could guess which coin produced each row (a row with 9 heads probably came from the more heads-happy coin).

But you have neither.

NoteWhat’s the difference between θ, θ_A, and θ_B?

is the bias of coin A — the probability a single flip of coin A lands heads (one number between 0 and 1). is the same for coin B. (no subscript) is just shorthand for both of them bundled together, the full parameter set:

So isn’t a third quantity with its own value — it’s the container. Writing means “given both biases at once.” (If we also wanted to learn how often each coin gets picked, we’d fold those mixing probabilities in too, but here we fix them at 50/50.)

The fix: guess, soften, repeat

Start with a guess, say , . Now instead of forcing a hard “this row is A” decision, EM does something gentler, alternating two steps:

E-step (Expectation). For each row, use the current guesses to compute the probability it came from each coin. The 9-heads row comes out to roughly 80% A / 20% B; the 4-heads row to roughly 35% A / 65% B. These soft probabilities are called responsibilities — coin A is “responsible” for 80% of the 9-heads row.

M-step (Maximization). Re-estimate the coins, but let every row contribute to both coins in proportion to those responsibilities. The 9-heads row dumps 80% of its 9 heads into A’s tally and 20% into B’s. It’s a weighted version of the trivial counting you’d do if labels were known — using soft counts instead of hard ones.

Then take the new ’s back to the E-step and go around again. Each loop the responsibilities sharpen and the estimates improve. The hidden labels and the parameters bootstrap each other into existence.

NoteHow is the 80/20 split actually computed?

It compares how well each coin explains that row’s outcome. For a row with heads out of flips, the responsibility of coin A is

(The prior 50/50 and the binomial coefficient cancel between top and bottom.) For the 9-heads row with , :

For the 4-heads row: , , giving . More heads tilts the ratio toward the heads-leaning coin; fewer heads tilts it toward the fair one.

NoteAre we solving for θ or for the hidden labels z?

We are solving for θ (the coin biases). That’s the answer you report: “coin A lands heads ~80% of the time.” The hidden labels (which coin made each row) are a nuisance — they only appear because they were never recorded. EM computes beliefs about them (the responsibilities) purely as an intermediate device to make the update possible. Think of the responsibilities as scaffolding and as the building: you keep rebuilding the scaffolding only because it lets you raise the building higher each round, and you throw it away at the end.

Why it keeps getting better: the surrogate-dome picture

Here’s the mental model that makes EM click. Picture the quality of a parameter guess as a height on a hill — the true likelihood of the data. You want the summit, but the hill is shrouded in fog: you can’t survey it or optimize it directly.

So at your feet you lay down a smooth dome — a simpler surrogate — engineered with two properties:

  1. it touches the hill exactly where you’re standing, and
  2. it never pokes above the hill anywhere.

You can easily find the top of the dome (that’s the M-step). Walk there. Because the dome never exceeded the hill, the hill at your new spot is at least as high as the dome’s peak — which was at least as high as where you started. So you’ve climbed the real hill without ever seeing it. Then you lay a fresh dome at the new spot and repeat.

That dome is the ELBO (Evidence Lower BOund), and the “touch then climb” rhythm is exactly the E-step and M-step. The fact that the dome stays below the hill is what makes climbing it safe — you can never be tricked into walking somewhere that looks good on the surrogate but is bad in reality.

The deeper “why” — why the dome touches where it does, why it stays below, and the inequality that guarantees the climb — is the heart of Part 2.

Watching it actually converge

Here’s EM run for real on the five experiments above, deliberately seeded off-target at , :

Two things worth noticing, both of which the theory predicts:

  • The log-likelihood (bottom panel) never decreases. That’s EM’s core guarantee — every round either improves the fit of the data or leaves it unchanged.
  • overshoots. It jumps up to on the first step, then drifts back down to . That’s allowed: EM only promises the overall likelihood climbs monotonically, not that each individual parameter marches straight to its target. From a bad seed the first responsibilities are misjudged, so the first update over-corrects, and later rounds walk it back.

The run settles at , — the biases that best explain the five observed heads-counts.

The whole algorithm in one breath

Fill in, then re-fit. The E-step fills in the missing labels with their expected (soft) values given where you currently stand; the M-step re-fits the parameters as if those soft labels were the data. Because the fill-in depended on the old parameters, you fill in again at the new spot — and around you go, until nothing moves.

Continue to Part 2 — Math with Intuition →
